payloads.name: .env file is reachable on the web
payloads.description: The .env file (or one of its env-specific variants) is being served by the web layer. Usually means the file was placed in a public/ or static/ directory by mistake, or the server has no rule for hidden files. Whatever the cause: every secret in that file is now public.
payloads.fix_hint: Move the .env file out of any directory the web server serves. In Next.js, .env should live at the project root, never inside public/. Verify your Express static config doesn't expose dotfiles (express.static('public', { dotfiles: 'ignore' })). And rotate every credential that was ever in that file — assume it's burned.

payloads.name: .git directory is reachable on the web
payloads.description: The web server is exposing the .git directory, which lets an attacker reconstruct the entire codebase (and every secret ever committed) via git's object format. Most often happens when someone copies a repo into a public/ folder for deploy.
payloads.fix_hint: Never deploy with a .git directory in the served root. Configure your build pipeline to exclude .git from the deploy artifact, or rsync with --exclude=.git. Add a server rule that 404s any path under /.git/ as defense in depth. And rotate any secret that was ever committed — git history is forever.

payloads.name: package.json is reachable on the web
payloads.description: package.json shouldn't normally be on the public side, but when it is, it leaks the project's full dependency tree (versions and all). Attackers use this to map known-vulnerable versions and to find tools to chain.
payloads.fix_hint: Don't ship package.json to the web. Most frameworks already handle this; the bug shows up when someone copies the project into public/ or static/. Add a rule that blocks /package.json explicitly if you've seen this happen.

payloads.name: production source map ships full source
payloads.description: A .js.map served in production with sourcesContent populated lets anyone read your unminified source code, including any keys, comments, or internal structure. Source maps are great for debugging — terrible to ship.
payloads.fix_hint: Stop shipping source maps to production. In Next.js, set `productionBrowserSourceMaps: false` in next.config.js (default). In Vite, set `build.sourcemap: false`. If you need maps for error reporting (Sentry et al.), upload them to your error service and don't serve them from the CDN.

payloads.name: client bundle contains a hardcoded API key
payloads.description: An API key for Stripe, OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS, GitHub, or another provider is included in the public HTML / JS bundle. Anyone who view-sources the page can take it. This is almost always a mis-prefixed env var (e.g., NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_SECRET_KEY instead of STRIPE_SECRET_KEY).
payloads.fix_hint: Move the key to a server-only env var (drop the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix in Next.js, never put it in VITE_/REACT_APP_ prefixed vars). Make the key-holding call from a server route, not the browser. Then rotate the key immediately — assume it's already harvested by bots watching public bundles.

payloads.name: /api/config (or similar) returns server-side secrets
payloads.description: A 'give me the runtime config' endpoint that returns all of process.env, or even a curated config object that includes server-side secrets, lets anonymous users read those secrets directly.
payloads.fix_hint: Never return process.env from an API. If the client legitimately needs runtime config (e.g., a public stripe publishable key, a feature flag), return only the specific public-safe values, hand-picked. Treat the config endpoint like any other API: explicit allow-list, not whatever's in scope.

payloads.name: Firebase / Google service-account private key in the bundle
payloads.description: A PEM-formatted private key (RSA, EC, generic, or service-account JSON containing one) is embedded in a publicly-fetched response. Service accounts grant the holder full backend access; an attacker who lifts this key can read and write your whole Firestore / BigQuery / GCP project.
payloads.fix_hint: Service-account credentials must never reach the client. Move all admin-SDK code to a server route and call it from the browser via your own API. Rotate the leaked key in the GCP console immediately — anyone who's view-sourced your page in the meantime has it.

payloads.name: database dump / backup file reachable on the web
payloads.description: A SQL dump, sqlite database, or generic backup file is reachable via a guessable path. Names like /backup.sql, /db.dump, /database.sqlite show up in routine recon scans; if your app's data lives in one, expect it to be downloaded.
payloads.fix_hint: Move backups out of any web-served directory. Store them in cloud object storage with private ACLs. If you absolutely need backups on the host, put them outside the public root and 404 any path that looks backup-ish (/backup*, /db.*, /*.sql, /*.dump). Treat any leaked dump as a full data breach — credentials inside need rotating.

payloads.name: robots.txt enumerates secret routes
payloads.description: robots.txt entries that 'disallow' admin or internal paths are an accidental advertisement of where to look. Search engines obey them, but humans use them as a route map.
payloads.fix_hint: Don't list sensitive paths in robots.txt. Auth-gate them instead, and rely on real authorization to keep them inaccessible. If you need search engines to skip a section, gate it server-side; bots that ignore robots.txt are exactly the ones you care about.
